meta.source: Prueba Tecnica - Ingeniero Cloud Senior v2.0 (May 2026)
meta.golden_rule: Never mark a task 'done' until ALL its verifications pass AND its STUDY_GUIDE.md section is written. Each verification is a hard gate before the next step.
meta.documentation_policy: Documentation is NOT written up front. The docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md section for a task is authored ONLY while/after executing that task, as a closing step. Every section MUST record the DECISION TAKEN AND WHY (chosen option, rejected alternatives, and reasoning) - this is explicitly required because the live review asks the candidate to justify every decision.

task_schema.context: why this task exists and how it fits the platform
task_schema.verifications: exact command + expected output; gate before next step
task_schema.documentation: mandatory study-guide section to write; task is NOT done without it
task_schema.definition_of_done: verifications green AND stop_gate met AND documentation written

tasks.title: AWS Infrastructure Provisioning
tasks.context: The spec forbids kind/minikube/k3d. The cluster must run on real VMs. On AWS that means EC2. We provision 3 Ubuntu 24.04 instances (1 server + 2 agents) in the default VPC, each with an extra 80GB gp3 EBS for Longhorn, behind a locked-down security group. 3 nodes are required so Longhorn can replicate across workers and CNPG can place replicas with anti-affinity for the failover demo.

steps.action: Confirm CLI identity and create infra/ + IDs file
commands: aws sts get-caller-identity --profile juan-test --query Account --output text
mkdir -p infra docs && echo AWS_REGION=us-east-1 > infra/aws-ids.env

steps.action: Create SSH key pair, save PEM chmod 600
commands: aws ec2 create-key-pair --profile juan-test --key-name juan-test-key --query KeyMaterial --output text > ~/.ssh/juan-test-key.pem

steps.action: Create security group; allow 22/80/443 from admin IP, all traffic intra-SG
commands: aws ec2 create-security-group --profile juan-test --group-name k8s-lab-sg --description 'k8s lab' --vpc-id vpc-023b0ff014485f3c0 --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=security-group,Tags=[{Key=Project,Value=k8s-lab}]'
steps.note: Persist SG_ID to infra/aws-ids.env. Re-check admin IP (181.53.99.60) before running; it changes.

steps.action: Launch 1x t3.large server + 2x t3.xlarge agents with 30GB root + 80GB data EBS
Record public/private IPs into infra/aws-ids.env (SERVER_*, AGENT1_*, AGENT2_*)
Verify SSH reachability to all 3 nodes

acceptance_criteria: 3 EC2 instances in state 'running' tagged Project=k8s-lab
Each instance has a 30GB root + 80GB data EBS volume
Security group restricts 22/80/443 to admin IP and allows all intra-SG traffic
SSH works to all 3 public IPs with the new key

verifications.command: aws sts get-caller-identity --profile juan-test --query Account --output text
aws ec2 describe-key-pairs --profile juan-test --key-names juan-test-key --query 'KeyPairs[0].KeyName' --output text; test -f ~/.ssh/juan-test-key.pem && echo PEM_OK
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --profile juan-test --group-ids $SG_ID --query 'SecurityGroups[0].IpPermissions[].FromPort' --output text
aws ec2 describe-instances --profile juan-test --filters Name=tag:Project,Values=k8s-lab Name=instance-state-name,Values=running --query 'length(Reservations[].Instances[])' --output text
for ip in $SERVER_PUB $AGENT1_PUB $AGENT2_PUB; do ssh -i ~/.ssh/juan-test-key.pem -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new ubuntu@$ip 'hostname'; done

tasks.stop_gate: 3 instances running with extra EBS, SG locked down, SSH to all 3 works.

study_topics: EC2 vs containers-as-nodes (why the spec bans kind/k3d)
EBS gp3 volume types and Nitro device naming (sdf -> nvme1n1)
VPC default networking, subnets, security groups, source/dest check
Why 3 nodes (Longhorn replication, CNPG anti-affinity)
attention_points: Admin IP in the SG changes; lock SSH to it
Shell does not persist vars between calls; zsh does not word-split unquoted vars
Stop instances when idle to control cost (~$0.42/hr running)

what_if_removed.extra EBS: Longhorn would have no replicated disk and CNPG PVCs could not bind
what_if_removed.intra-SG all-traffic rule: k3s API/etcd/Calico VXLAN between nodes would be blocked and the cluster never forms

review_questions: What would happen if you used a single node?

tasks.definition_of_done: v0.0..v0.5 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P0' section written.

tasks.title: Block 1 - k3s Cluster Bootstrap
tasks.context: Bring up a production-faithful k3s cluster: embedded etcd (not SQLite), Calico CNI (Istio-compatible, full NetworkPolicy), flannel and traefik disabled because we replace them with Calico and the Istio Gateway API. ServiceLB (klipper) is kept ON: it publishes LoadBalancer services on the real node IP, which is the justified AWS alternative to MetalLB (AWS VPC is SDN, MetalLB L2 ARP VIPs do not route).

steps.action: OS prep on all 3 nodes: open-iscsi, iscsi_tcp module, ip_forward
Install k3s server (flannel off, traefik off, cluster-init for etcd, node-external-ip)
steps.note: Node will be NotReady until Calico is installed - this is EXPECTED.

steps.action: Join 2 agents using node-token and server private IP
commands: curl -sfL https://get.k3s.io | K3S_URL=https://$SERVER_PRIV:6443 K3S_TOKEN=<t> INSTALL_K3S_EXEC='agent --node-external-ip=<AGENT_PUB>' sh -

steps.action: Install Calico (tigera-operator) with pod CIDR 10.42.0.0/16 and VXLAN encapsulation
steps.note: VXLAN (not BGP/IPIP) because AWS blocks BGP.

steps.action: Fetch kubeconfig locally, rewrite server URL to public IP
LoadBalancer smoke test with whoami
commands: kubectl create deploy whoami --image=traefik/whoami; kubectl expose deploy whoami --port=80 --type=LoadBalancer

acceptance_criteria: 3 nodes in Ready state from a local kubectl
Calico pods Running, no CNI errors
A LoadBalancer service receives an EXTERNAL-IP (node IP) and answers HTTP
etcd is the datastore (not SQLite)

verifications.command: ssh <node> 'systemctl is-active iscsid; sysctl -n net.ipv4.ip_forward; lsblk | grep -c nvme1n1'
ssh server 'sudo systemctl is-active k3s'
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready node --all --timeout=180s; kubectl get pods -n calico-system
verifications.expected: all nodes Ready; calico pods Running

verifications.command: kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get svc whoami -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}'; curl -s http://$SERVER_PUB/ | head -1
verifications.expected: node IP assigned; HTTP response from whoami

tasks.stop_gate: 3x Ready + Calico Running + LoadBalancer answers. README documents k3s-vs-kubeadm and ServiceLB-vs-MetalLB.

study_topics: k3s vs kubeadm: architecture, components, tradeoffs
Embedded etcd vs SQLite datastore in k3s
CNI role; why Calico over Flannel (NetworkPolicy, Istio compat)
Calico VXLAN vs BGP/IPIP and why AWS forces VXLAN
MetalLB L2 (ARP) vs k3s ServiceLB/klipper; why MetalLB L2 fails on AWS SDN
Pod CIDR vs Service CIDR; node-external-ip purpose
attention_points: Node is NotReady until CNI is installed - know why and say it confidently
ServiceLB binds node ports - explain how the Gateway gets an external IP
We intentionally SKIP MetalLB - have the AWS SDN justification ready

what_if_removed.Calico: no pod networking; nodes stay NotReady, no pod-to-pod traffic, no NetworkPolicy
what_if_removed.ServiceLB: LoadBalancer services stay <pending>; Istio Gateway gets no external IP
what_if_removed.embedded etcd: single-point datastore, no HA control plane semantics

review_questions: Why k3s over kubeadm for this deployment?
What happens if you delete the CNI?
Why does MetalLB L2 not work on AWS and what did you use instead?

tasks.definition_of_done: v1.1..v1.6 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P1' section written.

tasks.title: Block 2 - cert-manager + local CA
tasks.context: cert-manager manages all cluster TLS. A self-signed root CA is created via two chained ClusterIssuers: a 'selfsigned' issuer bootstraps a root CA certificate, and a 'CA' issuer signs the real leaf certs from that root. Gateways reference these cert Secrets in their HTTPS listeners. The root CA must be importable into the OS/browser trust store so reviewers get no TLS warnings.

steps.action: Install cert-manager via Helm with CRDs
commands: helm install cert-manager jetstack/cert-manager -n cert-manager --create-namespace --set crds.enabled=true

steps.action: Create selfsigned-issuer -> root-ca Certificate (isCA) -> ca-issuer
commands: apply Certificate root-ca (isCA:true) in cert-manager ns

steps.action: Issue a test cert from ca-issuer; export ca.crt
commands: kubectl get secret root-ca-secret -o jsonpath ... > ca.crt

acceptance_criteria: Both ClusterIssuers Ready=True
root CA Secret exists (tls.crt/tls.key/ca.crt)
A leaf cert signed by the local CA issues successfully
ca.crt exported and import procedure documented

verifications.command: kubectl wait --for=condition=Available deploy -n cert-manager --all --timeout=120s
kubectl get clusterissuer
kubectl get certificate <test>; openssl x509 -in ca.crt -noout -subject
verifications.expected: test cert Ready=True; subject shows local CA

tasks.stop_gate: Both issuers Ready, root CA Secret present, test leaf cert issued, ca.crt exported.

study_topics: cert-manager objects: Issuer/ClusterIssuer, Certificate, CertificateRequest, Secret
selfsigned vs CA issuer; why the two-step chain
X.509 chain of trust; isCA, root vs leaf
How a Gateway HTTPS listener consumes a cert Secret
attention_points: ClusterIssuer (cluster-wide) vs Issuer (namespaced)
Importing the root CA into OS/browser trust to avoid warnings
Secret must live where the Gateway can read it (or use ReferenceGrant)

what_if_removed.cert-manager: no automated TLS; Gateways have no valid certs; HTTPS endpoints break/warn
what_if_removed.ca-issuer: no way to sign leaf certs from the local root

review_questions: Why two ClusterIssuers instead of one?
TLS termination at Gateway vs HTTPRoute - tradeoffs?

tasks.definition_of_done: v2.1..v2.3 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P2' section written.

tasks.title: Block 3 - Gateway API v1.4 + Istio
tasks.context: Replace Ingress with Gateway API v1.4 (Standard Channel). Install the CRDs first, then Istio (demo profile) which registers the GatewayClass 'istio' and provisions an ingress gateway Deployment+Service. A main Gateway exposes the ToDo app (HTTP:80 redirect -> HTTPS:443 with a local-CA cert). Platform listeners expose argocd/grafana/kiali/jaeger/longhorn .local. HTTPRoutes live in each app namespace and reference the Gateway via parentRefs; ReferenceGrant authorizes cross-namespace references.

steps.action: Install Gateway API Standard Channel CRDs v1.4.0
Install Istio demo profile (brings Jaeger/Kiali/Prometheus/Grafana addons)
Create main Gateway: :80 HTTP->HTTPS redirect, :443 HTTPS with CA cert
Add platform listeners/certs for argocd/grafana/kiali/jaeger/longhorn .local
Add ReferenceGrants for cross-namespace cert/route refs

acceptance_criteria: GatewayClass istio Accepted=True
Gateways Programmed=True
istio-ingressgateway has an EXTERNAL-IP (node IP)
HTTP redirects to HTTPS; HTTPS served with local-CA cert (no warning after CA import)

verifications.command: kubectl get crd gateways.gateway.networking.k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.spec.versions[?(@.storage)].name}'; kubectl get crd | grep -c gateway.networking.k8s.io
kubectl get gatewayclass istio -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status}'
kubectl get gateway <name> -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Programmed")].status}'; kubectl get svc -n istio-system istio-ingressgateway -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}'

tasks.stop_gate: GatewayClass Accepted, Gateways Programmed, ingress has external IP, HTTP->HTTPS works with CA cert.

study_topics: Gateway API role model: GatewayClass/Gateway/HTTPRoute/ReferenceGrant
Gateway API vs classic Ingress (why it replaces Ingress)
Standard vs Experimental channel; v1 resources
Istio as GatewayClass controller; ingress gateway provisioning
TLS termination at Gateway vs passthrough vs HTTPRoute
parentRefs and cross-namespace ReferenceGrant
attention_points: Install CRDs BEFORE Istio
Use Standard channel, not Experimental
Explain TLS-at-Gateway choice vs HTTPRoute
Each HTTPRoute lives in the app namespace and references the shared Gateway

what_if_removed.Gateway API CRDs: Istio cannot create Gateways; no north-south routing
what_if_removed.main Gateway: no entry point; app and UIs unreachable

review_questions: Difference between TLS termination at Gateway vs HTTPRoute?

tasks.definition_of_done: v3.1..v3.3 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P3' section written.

tasks.title: Block 4 - GitOps with ArgoCD (App of Apps + Sync Waves)
tasks.context: The Git repo is the single source of truth using the App-of-Apps pattern: a root Application manages child Applications (apps/, infra/, observability/). Sync Waves enforce ordering: CRDs+cert-manager first, then Istio, then storage, then DB, then app. Everything bootstrapped imperatively in P2-P3 is migrated into Git and reconciled by ArgoCD.

steps.action: Create private repo with apps/ infra/ observability/ bootstrap/ layout
commands: git init; push to private GitHub/GitLab; grant evaluator access

steps.action: Install ArgoCD in argocd ns; expose via HTTPRoute argocd.local
Root Application + child Apps with Sync Waves 0..5
commands: apply root Application; annotate children argocd.argoproj.io/sync-wave

steps.action: Migrate P2-P3 manifests into Git; demonstrate GitOps loop (change replicas)
commands: edit replicas in Git; argocd auto-sync

acceptance_criteria: All Applications Synced + Healthy
ArgoCD UI reachable at argocd.local over HTTPS (no port-forward)
Sync Waves order respected
A Git change auto-applies to the cluster

verifications.command: kubectl get pods -n argocd; curl -k https://argocd.local | head -1
argocd app list -o name | wc -l ; argocd app list
edit replicas in git, wait sync, kubectl get deploy <x> -o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}'
verifications.expected: matches the new git value with no manual apply

tasks.stop_gate: All Applications Synced+Healthy, GitOps loop demonstrated, no imperative leftovers.

study_topics: GitOps principles; declarative vs imperative
ArgoCD: Application, Project, sync policies, self-heal, prune
App-of-Apps pattern
Sync Waves and hooks for ordering
Drift detection and auto-sync
attention_points: HTTPRoutes/Gateways must be Git-managed, not manual
Sync Wave order: CRDs+cert-manager -> Istio -> Longhorn -> CNPG -> observability -> app
Be ready to show a live drift->reconcile

what_if_removed.ArgoCD: no continuous reconciliation; manual drift; loses single-source-of-truth
what_if_removed.Sync Waves: race conditions: app deploys before CRDs/DB exist and fails

review_questions: How do Sync Waves guarantee order?
What happens if someone kubectl-edits a resource ArgoCD manages?

tasks.definition_of_done: v4.2..v4.4 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P4' section written.

tasks.title: Block 5 - Local Container Registry (ECR simulated)
tasks.context: Deploy an in-cluster registry (Docker Registry v2 or Zot) that simulates a private ECR. Images are built locally, pushed to the cluster registry, and referenced by Deployments. The registry must be reachable from the nodes for image pulls.

steps.action: Deploy registry (Deployment+Service, persistent volume)
Build, push, and pull-test an image
commands: docker build; docker push <registry>/img; kubectl run test --image=<registry>/img

acceptance_criteria: Registry pod Running and reachable from nodes
An image pushed to it can be pulled by a pod

verifications.command: kubectl get pods -l app=registry; curl -s http://<registry>/v2/_catalog
kubectl run test --image=/ --restart=Never; kubectl get pod test

tasks.stop_gate: Image pushed to cluster registry and pulled by a pod.

study_topics: OCI Distribution Spec; registry v2 API
Docker Registry v2 vs Zot vs Harbor
imagePullSecrets and insecure registries
Real ECR auth token 12h expiry problem
attention_points: Justify registry choice in README
Node trust for insecure/HTTP registry (k3s registries.yaml)

what_if_removed.registry: Deployments cannot pull app images; pods stuck ImagePullBackOff

review_questions: How would you solve ECR's 12h token expiry?

tasks.definition_of_done: v5.1..v5.2 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P5' section written.

tasks.title: Block 6 - ToDo App (2 microservices)
tasks.context: todo-api (Go1.22/Node20, :8080) is a REST CRUD service on PostgreSQL exposing /healthz, /readyz, /metrics; it must propagate Istio tracing headers (x-request-id, x-b3-*) on outbound calls so Jaeger shows full traces. todo-frontend (React18+Nginx, :3000) calls the API through the Gateway. Both run in ns todo with Istio sidecars. Full set of K8s hygiene resources is required.

steps.action: Implement todo-api: CRUD + health/ready/metrics + tracing header propagation
Implement todo-frontend SPA calling API via Gateway
K8s resources: Deployment(2,RollingUpdate), HTTPRoute, HPA(60%,2,5), probes, requests/limits, NetworkPolicy, ConfigMap+Secret, ServiceAccount per svc
Build + push images to local registry

acceptance_criteria: Both services 2/2 Ready (app+sidecar)
All required K8s resources present
Rolling update has zero downtime
Create/list/edit/delete task works end to end and persists in PostgreSQL
NetworkPolicy restricts frontend->api and api->PG only

verifications.command: curl /readyz (DB down vs up); curl /metrics
verifications.expected: /readyz 200 only when DB reachable; /metrics returns Prometheus text

verifications.command: kubectl get deploy,hpa,networkpolicy,sa,cm,secret -n todo; kubectl get pods -n todo
kubectl rollout restart deploy/todo-api -n todo while curling endpoint
create task in frontend; kubectl exec psql 'select * from tasks'

tasks.stop_gate: End-to-end CRUD works, all pods 2/2, zero-downtime rollout, NetworkPolicy enforced.

study_topics: Liveness vs readiness probes semantics
HPA: metrics, target utilization, min/max, scaling behavior
RollingUpdate strategy, maxSurge/maxUnavailable
NetworkPolicy with Calico (default-deny + allow rules)
ConfigMap vs Secret; env injection
Istio sidecar injection; B3 trace header propagation
ServiceAccount and least privilege RBAC
attention_points: readyz must actually check DB connectivity
Without header propagation Jaeger traces fragment
2/2 means app + Envoy sidecar

what_if_removed.readiness probe: traffic routed to not-ready pods -> errors during rollout
what_if_removed.NetworkPolicy: any pod can reach PG; no segmentation

tasks.definition_of_done: v6.1..v6.5 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P6' section written.

tasks.title: Block 7 - PostgreSQL with CloudNativePG
tasks.context: CloudNativePG operator manages a 3-instance Postgres cluster (1 primary + 2 read replicas) with automatic failover. DB tododb, owner creds in a Secret, storage on Longhorn (>=5Gi/instance), built-in PodMonitor for Prometheus. todo-api connects to the operator-generated RW Service using the CNPG Secret.

steps.action: Install CNPG operator in cnpg-system
Define Cluster: 3 instances, tododb, Longhorn storage 5Gi, PodMonitor
Wire todo-api to CNPG RW Service via CNPG Secret env
Failover demo: delete primary pod

acceptance_criteria: Cluster Healthy with 3 instances
Failover < 30s with no manual action
Data persists across todo-api pod restarts
PG metrics scraped by Prometheus / visible in Grafana

verifications.command: kubectl get cluster -n <ns>; kubectl get pods -n <ns>
note current primary; kubectl delete pod <primary>; watch kubectl get cluster
create tasks; kubectl delete pod -l app=todo-api; re-list tasks

tasks.stop_gate: 3 instances Healthy, failover <30s, persistence proven, metrics flowing.

study_topics: Operator pattern and CRDs
Postgres streaming replication, primary/replica roles
CNPG failover/switchover; RW/RO/R services
PodMonitor vs ServiceMonitor
How storageClass binds to Longhorn PVCs
attention_points: App must use the -rw service, not a pod IP
Creds come from the CNPG-generated Secret
Failover must beat 30s - know the mechanism

what_if_removed.CNPG operator: no managed failover/backups; manual Postgres ops
what_if_removed.RW service: app cannot find the current primary after failover

review_questions: What happens to writes during failover?

tasks.definition_of_done: v7.2..v7.5 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P7' section written.

tasks.title: Block 8 - Persistent Storage (Longhorn)
tasks.context: Longhorn provides distributed, replicated block storage so PostgreSQL data survives pod/node/cluster restarts. Requires open-iscsi (installed in P1.1) and uses the extra 80GB EBS on each node. Default 2 replicas per volume; UI exposed via HTTPRoute longhorn.local.

steps.action: Prepare extra EBS as Longhorn disk on each node
commands: mkfs/mount /dev/nvme1n1 or set as Longhorn disk path

steps.action: Install Longhorn (Helm), defaultReplicaCount=2, UI HTTPRoute
Verify CNPG PVCs Bound and volumes replicated/Healthy

acceptance_criteria: Longhorn installed before CNPG
CNPG PVCs Bound
Volumes Healthy + replicated (>=2) in Longhorn UI
Data survives delete+recreate of Postgres pods

verifications.command: ssh <node> 'lsblk | grep nvme1n1'
verifications.expected: extra disk present on all nodes

verifications.command: kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system; curl -k https://longhorn.local | head -1
kubectl get pvc -A | grep cnpg; check Longhorn UI volume health

tasks.stop_gate: PVCs Bound, volumes Healthy/replicated, persistence proven.

study_topics: CSI and dynamic provisioning
Longhorn architecture: manager, engine, replicas
open-iscsi requirement and why
StorageClass, PV, PVC binding lifecycle
Replica count and node failure tolerance
attention_points: Install Longhorn BEFORE CNPG (dependency)
2 replicas needs 2 schedulable workers
open-iscsi must be active on every node

what_if_removed.Longhorn: no replicated storage; CNPG PVCs cannot bind; data not durable

review_questions: What happens if a node holding a replica dies?

tasks.definition_of_done: v8.1..v8.3 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P8' section written.

tasks.title: Block 9 - Observability (Istio + Prometheus/Grafana/Jaeger/Kiali/Loki)
tasks.context: Istio sidecars capture L7 metrics and distributed traces for ns todo. kube-prometheus-stack scrapes cluster/app/CNPG metrics; Grafana visualizes; Jaeger shows trace chains Gateway->todo-api->PostgreSQL; Kiali shows the live mesh graph; Loki centralizes logs. All UIs exposed via HTTPRoutes (no port-forward at review).

steps.action: Enable sidecar injection in todo, restart pods
commands: kubectl label ns todo istio-injection=enabled; rollout restart

steps.action: Install kube-prometheus-stack + Loki/Promtail; Grafana HTTPRoute
commands: helm install both; apply HTTPRoute grafana.local

steps.action: Expose Jaeger/Kiali via HTTPRoutes
Import dashboards 7639 (Istio), 315 (K8s), CNPG
Add >=1 VirtualService (timeout/retry on todo-api)

acceptance_criteria: All todo pods 2/2
Grafana/Jaeger/Kiali reachable via HTTPS HTTPRoutes
Jaeger shows full span chain per endpoint
Kiali shows live traffic + p50/p95
Dashboards show real data

verifications.command: kubectl get pods -n todo
curl -k https://grafana.local | head -1; Prometheus targets Up
generate traffic; open Jaeger; inspect a GET /tasks trace

tasks.stop_gate: Traces correlated, Kiali graph live, Grafana dashboards with real data, all UIs via HTTPRoute.

study_topics: Three pillars: metrics, logs, traces
Istio telemetry; Envoy sidecar; mTLS
Prometheus scraping, ServiceMonitor/PodMonitor
Distributed tracing, B3 propagation, spans
Kiali graph and VirtualService (timeout/retry)
Loki/Promtail and LogQL basics
attention_points: Header propagation from P6 is what makes traces whole
All UIs via HTTPRoute, NOT port-forward at review
Be able to read a Jaeger trace live

what_if_removed.Istio sidecar: no L7 metrics/traces/mTLS for the app

review_questions: What does the VirtualService do and why?

tasks.definition_of_done: v9.1..v9.6 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P9' section written.

tasks.title: Block 10 - Public Exposure (Ngrok)
tasks.context: Expose the ToDo frontend publicly via the Ngrok Kubernetes Operator (manages tunnels as native K8s resources, integrates with GitOps, no host process). A shared public URL lets reviewers create a task remotely that persists in PostgreSQL.

steps.action: Install Ngrok K8s Operator (GitOps), tunnel to todo-frontend
commands: helm install ngrok-operator; apply tunnel CR / Gateway

steps.action: Verify public URL and end-to-end write
commands: open URL; create task; check PG

acceptance_criteria: Operator pods Running
A public URL is issued and serves the frontend
Task created from the public URL persists in PostgreSQL

verifications.command: kubectl get pods -n ngrok-operator; get the issued public URL
open public URL, create task, kubectl exec psql select

tasks.stop_gate: App reachable from public URL; remote write persists.

study_topics: Reverse tunnels and how Ngrok exposes private services
Ngrok Operator CRDs vs binary vs Cloudflare Tunnel
Free-tier limits (1 tunnel, URL rotates)
attention_points: Document URL-changes-per-session behavior
Operator integrates with GitOps (no host process)

what_if_removed.Ngrok: app only reachable inside the VPC; no public demo URL

review_questions: How does Ngrok expose a private service without firewall changes?

tasks.definition_of_done: v10.1..v10.2 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P10' section written.

tasks.title: Deliverables & Live-Review Prep
tasks.context: Package the deliverables: README (prereqs, from-zero steps, per-block design decisions, /etc/hosts list, k3s-vs-kubeadm justification), bootstrap.sh/Makefile with per-block targets, clean commit history, evaluator repo access. Dry-run the 11-point live review checklist.

steps.action: Write README with all required sections
Create Makefile/bootstrap.sh targets: cluster infra gateway argocd observability expose
Clean commit history; grant evaluator access
Dry-run the 11-point review checklist

acceptance_criteria: README lets a fresh reader reproduce from zero
Make targets run idempotently
All 11 review demonstrations pass
Repo access granted to evaluator

verifications.command: make cluster infra gateway argocd observability expose (on a clean attempt or dry-run)
walk the 11 checklist items end to end

tasks.stop_gate: README complete, Make targets work, all 11 review items pass, evaluator has access.

study_topics: The 11-point live review checklist and its evidence
For each component: purpose + what-if-removed (review explicitly asks this)
Overall architecture narrative end to end
attention_points: Documentation consultation during review is allowed and expected
Be able to explain every design decision
Practice the failover and GitOps-loop demos live

what_if_removed.README: evaluator cannot reproduce; loses reproducibility points

review_questions: Why each tool over its alternatives?

tasks.definition_of_done: v11.2,v11.4 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P11' section written.

tasks.title: Bonus - mTLS strict + HPA load test (+10)
tasks.context: Selected high-value/low-effort bonus: (1) PeerAuthentication STRICT in ns todo to force mTLS for all internal traffic; (2) generate load on todo-api with k6/hey and prove the HPA scales pods up under demand and back down.

steps.action: Apply PeerAuthentication STRICT in todo
Run k6/hey load against todo-api; watch HPA
commands: k6 run load.js; watch kubectl get hpa

acceptance_criteria: mTLS STRICT enforced (plaintext blocked)
HPA scales replicas up under load, then back down

verifications.command: istioctl authn tls-check; attempt plaintext call to a todo pod
run load; watch kubectl get hpa -n todo -w
verifications.expected: replicas scale toward max, then back to min after load stops

tasks.stop_gate: mTLS STRICT verified and HPA autoscaling demonstrated.

study_topics: Istio PeerAuthentication PERMISSIVE vs STRICT
mTLS handshake in the mesh
HPA scaling algorithm and stabilization windows
Load testing with k6/hey
attention_points: STRICT can break clients without sidecars - know the blast radius
HPA needs metrics-server / Prometheus adapter

what_if_removed.PeerAuthentication STRICT: internal traffic may fall back to plaintext

review_questions: How does the HPA decide to scale?

tasks.definition_of_done: v12.1,v12.2 green AND stop_gate met AND docs/STUDY_GUIDE.md 'P12' section written.
